question: A server uses a periodic timer (every 30 seconds) that calls waitpid(-1, &status, WNOHANG) in a loop to collect exited children. Under load spikes, thousands of children exit within a few seconds, but ps shows most remain in Z state for up to 30 seconds before being reaped. At peak, the process table fills and fork fails with EAGAIN. Why does a polling-based reaping strategy create this failure mode, and what is the minimal change to eliminate zombie accumulation latency?
answer: Polling creates a reaping gap proportional to the timer interval. Children exiting between polls stay zombie, and burst accumulation exceeds pid_max (32768 on Linux). SIGCHLD coalesces multiple exits into one delivery, so a single waitpid misses children. The fix is a SIGCHLD handler looping waitpid with WNOHANG until it returns 0 or ECHILD. signalfd provides a non-signal alternative with epoll-based edge detection, removing both the timer gap and signal handler restrictions.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  P[Parent process] --> F[fork creates child]
  F --> C[Child runs and calls _exit]
  C --> Z[Kernel marks zombie state]
  Z --> T{Parent reaping strategy?}
  T -->|30-second timer polling| Z30[Zombie persists up to 30 seconds]
  Z30 --> A[Zombies accumulate at rate × interval]
  A --> PIDs[PID table fills. fork returns EAGAIN]
  T -->|SIGCHLD handler with WNOHANG loop| R[Reaped immediately: latency < 1ms]
  R --> F
  Z --> S{Kernel action}
  S -->|SIGCHLD sent| H[Handler sets atomic flag]
  H --> L[Loop waitpid-1 status WNOHANG until 0 or ECHILD]
  L --> R
  S -->|SIG_IGN or SA_NOCLDWAIT| AR[Auto-reap, exit status discarded]
  AR --> F
